responseSet.title: The role of the Revenge Cycle in the Conflict in Gaza
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The role of the Revenge Cycle in the Conflict in Gaza

David Lotto

David Lotto

Email: dlotto@nycap.rr.com

David Lotto  

Gaza, revenge, narcissistic rage, Kohut

This paper examines the psychological roots of the war in Gaza using Kohut's theory of narcissistic injury and the vengeful reaction it engenders. This is followed by an exploration of certain aspects of revenge, including some brain imaging findings, and its relationship to justice. The importance of humiliation in feeding the cycle of narcissistic injury leading to revenge, leading to further humiliation, leading to further revenge, is discussed. The cycle can be self-perpetuating as we are currently witnessing in Gaza.

[[RunningHead]]

Dissociation and Trauma in the Context of Mental Health Responses for those Populations Affected by Severe Adversity and Suffering During Warfare: Adopting a Socio-Cultural Perspective on Israel-Palestine

Anne Jennings

Anne Jennings

Essex, UK
Email: a.m.jennings@talk21.com

Anne Jennings  

warfare, dissociation, trauma, mental health, socio-cultural context

In this short article, I consider questions of dissociation and trauma for populations facing severe adverse situations within warzones, focussing on Israel-Palestine. As Psychoanalytic Psychotherapists interested in global mental health, attention to the human conditions and safety needed for clinical interventions in war-torn and occupied regions is paramount. Consideration of the cultural lens through which we view psychological distress, will help us to respond constructively and sensitively to the mental health needs of all those directly affected by the conflict. This article focusses on the realities for Palestinian clinicians working to support mental health in Gaza, who speak to the importance of understanding the political and social situation that gives rise to suffering in Palestine and the need for a culturally informed response to the mass psychological suffering of Palestinian communities. Such a widening of our understanding as a discipline will help us to respond constructively and sensitively to the mental health needs of all peoples affected. We may also be encouraged to assume a more active humanitarian stance in supporting the cessation of killing by calling for an immediate ceasefire to enable all communities to exist and to thrive.

[[RunningHead]]

With so Many Conflicts, Why have a Special issue on the War in Israel/Palestine?

John, Lord Alderdice

John, Lord Alderdice

Email: john.alderdice@hmc.ox.ac.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4322-0049

John, Lord Alderdice  

The world is currently living through the most dangerous period of war and violent political conflict since the end of the Second World War. Indeed, with the advent of hypersonic weapons, able to carry a nuclear payload, and potentially managed by artificial intelligence, this has become the most dangerous period in human history. For the psychoanalytic world too, it is a time of deep division and profound danger.

Prior to the First World War, Freud and his colleagues focussed largely on the material that emerged in individual psychoanalytical work, with the sexual instinct foremost. However, such was the upheaval and torment across Europe during the 1914-1918 war that they had to pay attention to the psychology of societal disturbance and, going ‘beyond the pleasure principle’, developed psychoanalytic theory to address the aggressive drive, and postulated a death instinct. In the years that followed more was written about the societal applications of psychoanalysis, but then the world was plunged into a second global cataclysm. There were many horrors, not least the ending of the war with the first use of nuclear weapons, but the awfulness of the Holocaust had a very particular impact on psychoanalysts and their professional communities because many of them were Jewish. Some survived in continental Europe but many more fled to Britain, the USA and elsewhere for safety – Freud and his daughter Anna, seeking refuge in London. After such terror and trauma many psychoanalysts turned away from thinking so much about societal relations, focussing entirely on their clinical work, as a reaction to their terrible experiences. They had learned what can happen in the larger society when dangerous movements develop with the terrifying and seemingly irresistible destructive force of a tsunami.

For some Jews these traumas gave momentum to the Zionist movement that sought to create a state which would be a safe and secure place for Jews. Despite the exile having lasted for almost two thousand years, the emotional pull of the ancient historical home in Palestine was so great that it overcame any rational considerations of whether this small sliver of land in the Middle East was likely to be a truly secure space. responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

London, UK
paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

This special issue focuses on the conflict between Israel and Gaza. Whilst at the time of writing this editorial we are in the early days of a ceasefire, there is a widely held fear that this will not be the end of the conflict. Similarly, although the horrific events of 7/10/23 were the catalyst for this most recent episode, they were certainly not the beginning of the conflict.

According to Wikipedia (2025), ‘as of 8 January, over 47,000 people − 45,936 Palestinian and 1,706 Israeli – have been reported killed in the Gaza War, as well as 166 journalists and media workers, 120 academics, and over 224 humanitarian aid workers, a number that includes 179 employees of UNWRA. Scholars have estimated 80% of Palestinians killed are civilians, while a study by OCHR, that verified fatalities from three independent sources, found that 70% of Palestinians killed were women and children’. Tragically, research published in the Lancet suggests that the Palestinian death toll could be 55,298–78,525, substantially higher than the official figures reported by the Hamas-run health ministry (Jamaluddine et al., 2025).

In addition to this unimaginable human cost, the war has revealed deep divisions within the global community. Although most Western governments have supported Israel, there has also been extensive pro-Palestinian activism within these countries. These divisions have also been apparent within our profession, causing rifts between patient and clinician, between colleagues and within our professional organisations. Divisions are not uncommon in our work but this conflict, more than any other, seems to lead to such extreme polarisation that we become unable to think together. Thought and speech are shut down by accusations of hatred or prejudice. This journal has experienced such dynamics, both between colleagues and between the journal and its readership. These challenges to thinking and the policing of discourse were one of the factors that led us to conclude that the conflict between Israel and Gaza, rather than conflicts in general, would be an appropriate subject for a special issue of this journal. But, more importantly, it would have been collusive to remain silent during a conflict that is alleged to involve war crimes and genocide.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Evil: A Psychoanalytic Perspective on the Hamas Attacks of 7 October 2023

Aner Govrin

Aner Govrin

Ramat Gan, Israel
Email: govrina.biu@gmail.com

Aner Govrin  

Evil, victim, perpetrator, conflict, Israel-Palestine conflict

This paper examines the Hamas attacks of 7 October 2023, through the lens of several theories of evil. It applies frameworks from Christopher Bollas, Ruth Stein, Sue Grand, Robert J. Lifton, and Aner Govrin to explore the psychological underpinnings of this act of terror. The study employs an interpretative approach to analyze the attacks, considering the perpetrators’ backgrounds and the sociopolitical context. Key concepts explored include Bollas's ‘killed self’, Stein's analysis of religious justification for violence, Grand's ‘malignant dissociative contagion’, Lifton's theory of totalism, and Govrin's prototype of evil perception. The paper discusses how these theories illuminate the role of trauma, ideology, and psychological distortion in fostering extreme violence. It also considers the ethical implications of applying psychoanalytic theories to large-scale events and acknowledges the limitations of this approach. The analysis reveals common threads across theories, including the impact of psychological damage, disconnection from empathy, and the interplay of love and hate in acts of evil. The paper concludes by emphasizing the importance of recognizing suffering on all sides of the conflict while maintaining a clear moral stance against acts of terror. It calls for further interdisciplinary research to inform strategies for conflict resolution and prevention of future atrocities.

[[RunningHead]]

Transitioning from Soldier's Matrix to the Development of Negative Capability

Anna Zajenkowska , Agnieszka Chrzczonowicz-Stępień and Katarzyna Czajkowska-Łukasiewicz

Katarzyna Czajkowska-Łukasiewicz

Warsaw, Poland
Email: zajenkowska@gmail.com

Warsaw, Poland

Katarzyna Czajkowska-Łukasiewicz  

Negative capability, regressed relations, chosen traumas, collective narcissism, social identity theory, narcissistic injuries and rage, soldier's matrix

In the 21st century, the concept of negative capability—defined as the capacity to tolerate uncertainty and engage in deep reflection rather than reacting impulsively—has become increasingly relevant. This paper explores how transgenerational trauma, particularly in the context of Israeli-Palestinian and other global conflicts, undermines this crucial capability. Such trauma often leads to narcissistic injuries and rage, further eroding negative capability at both individual and collective levels. We examine the pervasive feelings of insecurity and threat, introducing the concept of the “soldier's matrix,” a psychological framework driven by these emotions. This sets the stage for an analysis of reactions to traumatic events, linking narcissistic dynamics with deeper relational traumas. To clarify this complex interplay, we draw on concepts such as regressed relations, chosen traumas, collective narcissism, and social identity theory. We also focus on diagnosing the factors that hinder mentalization and obstruct the restoration of negative capability. Finally, we propose a comprehensive three-layered intervention for conflicted parties, emphasizing the need to rebuild reflective spaces through education, international cooperation, and leadership from political and social figures.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Psychoanalytic Reflections on the Events in Gaza

David Bell

David Bell

London, UK
Email: davidlbell@hotmail.com

David Bell  

politics, Palestine, psychoanalysis, Israel

This paper explores the different ways in which psychoanalysis can engage with socio-political events, taking as its primary focus the conflict between Israel and Palestine/Gaza. At a general level psychoanalysis shares with other disciplines a mode of thought which emphasises such things as historical continuity, the distinction between what is manifest and the hidden realities it obscures. More specifically psychoanalytic contribution comes from its understanding of the nature and functioning of unconscious phantasy both at group and individual level. The paper problematises the frequent misuse of the term ‘psychoanalytic neutrality’ which, the author claims, serves to silence opposition to a dominant ideology rather than question it. Lastly, the author describes how attempts to think about the onset of the current conflict in its long historical and geopolitical context are misunderstood as seeking to provide justification- the author emphasises the need to maintain the critical distinction between these two modalities of thought.

[[RunningHead]]

When Truth is Not Shared

Dana Amir

Dana Amir

Haifa, Israel
Email: dana.amir2@gmail.com

Dana Amir  

trauma, evil, minus links, forgiveness, Bion, truth

Political violence has, for a very long time, been a focus of academic interest in various disciplines, probing the relentlessly cyclic nature of violence and counter-violence. This essay looks at the Israeli-Palestinian conflict with reference to the map of links by means of which Bion conceptualized the relations between two individuals, giving special attention to how negative configurations of these links impede contact with truth on both sides. Relating to theoretical writings by Baudrillard, Benjamin, and Arendt, this essay suggests replacing planar with spatial thinking and the condition of revenge with that of forgiveness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vulnerable Encounters: Identities and their Discontents

Yael Bankirer

Yael Bankirer

Email: yaelbankirer@gmail.com

Yael Bankirer  

Israel-Gaza, trauma, witnessing, mutual vulnerability, identities

In this paper, I try to articulate my psychoanalytic-political-emotional experience as an Israeli psychoanalyst working in England during this painful and challenging time. With a growing awareness of how difficult it is to hold an analytic space in the face of traumatic violence, I analyse the places in which dialogues collapse into a binary framework, both in my clinical encounters and in the discourse itself. Connecting Amir's concept of the Inner Witness, Benjamin's understanding of the Moral Third, and Butler's Compass of Mourning, I aim to explore vulnerabilities beyond the binary structure of doer and done-to, victim and aggressor. Recognising a repeated denial of pain and loss at the heart of our discourse, I argue for a political-psychoanalytic stance that can hold a more complex picture by agreeing to witness.

Poetry and Re-Imagining in a Time of Crisis

‘Changing the Very Terms of the Dilemma’

Mary Adams

Mary Adams

London, UK
Email: Mary.adams@btinternet.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2330-0992

Mary Adams  

Re-imagining, Palestine, Nakba, Heaney, Eghbariah

The author compares the power of imagery in poetry with the unconscious images and re-imagining that allow us to see things in a new light in psychotherapy. Palestinian poetry illuminates their world and the devastation happening in Gaza in the way dreams capture our inner world. Emphasis is given to the role of the imagination in effecting social change, seen remarkably in the ‘Good Friday Agreement’ in Northern Ireland. The poetry of Seamus Heaney, for example, is linked with a fundamental perceptual shift around issues of national sovereignty. In his long-term support for Palestine, Heaney looked to poetry to redress the injustice and to counter the silencing and ‘turning a blind eye’. Nakba denial is seen as attacking the very life sustaining act of re-imagining.

Confronting the Inhuman

Paul Hoggett

Paul Hoggett

Bristol, UK
Email: paul.hoggett@uwe.ac.uk

Paul Hoggett  

indifference, cruelty, ecocide, genocide, identitarianism

This article uses the lens of both Gaza and the climate crisis to examine some aspects of inhumanity. In both situations, we observe a puzzling indifference to ongoing manifestations of human destructiveness. The article explores the dynamics of disavowal, the complex nature of racism, and the tragic dimension of some political conflicts. In conclusion, some of the psychological and material forces involved in the resurgence of authoritarian nationalism are considered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Brief Report: Private Pleasures and the Damage Done: Asking the Unasked Question about Consumption of Online Pornography

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
Email: alemma@mac.com

Alessandra Lemma  

online pornography, sexuality, harm, mentalisation of sexual desire

The domestication of the Internet has invigorated debate about the harms of pornography. The difference between pre-Internet and online pornography is not in any straightforward sense only one of degree. Compared to pre-Internet pornography, speed, and ease of access to an extensive range of pornographic content, along with the consumption of pornography in a virtual context, have introduced new prudential risks. In this Brief Report I restrict my focus to the impact on sexual health and on personal relationships and summarise the relevant research. Drawing on a psychoanalytically informed conceptual framework, I argue that the online medium changes, in prudentially significant ways, the consumer’s relationship to the sexual materials by providing a virtual space within which sexual desire is gratified quickly and non-reflectively, undermining the consumer’s capacity a) to mentalise their sexual desire and that of the other and b) to evaluate the prudential risks associated with the consumption of online pornography. The risks are especially significant for the digital generation whose sexual development is now more likely to be shaped by online pornography. I conclude by outlining one key practice implication arising from the research.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When Florence Met Freud: Interaction and Intersection between Psychoanalysis and Nursing

Sarah M. Ramsey

Sarah M. Ramsey

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8068-8439

Moorside Rd, Davyhulme, Manchester M41 5SL, UK
Email: sarah.ramsey@mft.nhs.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8068-8439

Sarah M. Ramsey  

psychoanalysis, nursing, healthcare, psychosocial

A theoretical paper which considers the previous multiple attempts by nursing to engage with and usefully apply the theories of psychoanalysis to varying aspects of the nursing role. References to psychoanalysis within the nursing literature of the last century are reviewed and the legitimacy of the use of psychoanalytic theory ‘outside the clinic’ explored with reference to general hospital nursing in the UK’s National Health Service. Themes explored include unconscious motivation behind nursing as a career choice, with associated risks of stress and burnout when the unconscious drive to heal is thwarted. Psychoanalytic consideration of nurse-patient relationships provides insight into the patient experience on hospital wards, whilst exploration of other aspects of the nursing role was found to be limited. From a wider perspective, psychoanalysis offers nursing an insight into societal changes impacting the profession. The systems and processes of the healthcare institution have also been explored, particularly in relation to containment of anxiety, though limited practical impact has resulted from the psychoanalytic insight encountered in the literature. The limited engagement between psychoanalysis and general nursing is considered from both a psychoanalytic and nursing perspective; ongoing potentiality for enriching dialogue between the disciplines is established and potential barriers explored.

Evaluating the 20-Day Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) Training Programme: A Mixed Methods Investigation to Identify Characteristics Associated with Successful Outcomes of DIT

Jennifer Khalastchy , Deborah Abrahams , Rob Saunders and Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

London, UK
Email: jennykhala@yahoo.com

London, UK

London, UK

Peter Fonagy  

Dynamic interpersonal therapy, depression, brief psychodynamic therapy, psychoanalytic/dynamic competences, thematic analysis

Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) is a brief psychodynamic therapy for depression, developed from the fundamental competences of evidence-based psychoanalytic/dynamic therapeutic methods. The 20-Day DIT training programme at the Anna Freud Centre in London is offered for clinicians who do not meet these psychoanalytic/dynamic competences. 28 therapists, each with three patients, underwent the 20-Day DIT training in 2019. The current study evaluated the 20-Day DIT training by investigating factors associated with the successful patient outcomes of this cohort. Reliable recovery and reliable improvement of patients was 69% and 90%, respectively. Quantitative analyses found that patients who have positive outcomes were likely to have lower baseline Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) scores and lower patient complexity. A thematic analysis of the therapists’ perspectives suggested that the most important elements of DIT perceived to be associated with successful patient outcomes cluster around four superordinate themes: aspects of the DIT model/training, characteristics of the patient, aspects of the patient-therapist relationship, and characteristics of the therapist. The results suggest that the DIT protocol can be acquired by novice therapists in psychoanalytic/dynamic methods and offer insight into the factors that may be associated with successful outcomes.

Testing Heinz Kohut’s thoughts on Narcissism and Narcissistic Rage: Narcissistic Injury Paves the Way for Radicalization and Subclinical Paranoid States – An Experimental Study

Klaus Michael Reininger , Hannah Marie Biel , Timo Hennig , Steffen Moritz , Peer Briken and Bernd Löwe

Bernd Löwe

Hamburg, Germany
Martinistr. 52, Hamburg D-20246, Germany
m.reininger@uke.uni-hamburg.de
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-6671-0465

Hamburg, Germany

Potsdam, Germany

http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4220-3378

Hamburg, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4220-3378

Bernd Löwe  

Radicalization, state paranoia, narcissism, narcissistic injury, narcissistic rage

According to psychoanalytic self-psychological theory, narcissistic injuries may induce radical intentions, and subclinical paranoid states via self-psychological mechanisms. The testing took place in a narcissistic-injuring situation in Germany, where a reform of psychotherapy-training was implemented with only brief period of transition for those aiming to become psychotherapists after graduating. Within a randomized controlled-experimental design, 100 psychology-students were to read information about short transition-periods for them (affectedness-by-the-reform-condition) vs. for education-students (no-affectedness-by-the-reform-condition). After reading a radicalization-prompt, discussing in a 30-minute-group-discussion, participants in both conditions completed dependent measures of separatist identification, negative affects, overconfidence, radical intentions and subclinical paranoid states. Compared to the control condition (n = 49), psychologists in the experimental-condition (n = 51) stronger experienced narcissistic injury (d between 0.79 and 1.01; manipulation-check). They experienced a stronger alloyance with an archaic-omnipotent object (d = 0.52), exhibited more negative affects (d = 0.46), claimed overconfidence (d = 0.50), more radical intentions (d = 0.52), and subclinical paranoid states (d = 0.43). Our study supports Kohut’s idea that antisocial as well as paranoid states are responses to narcissistic injuries mediated by the alloyance with an archaic-omnipotent object, negative affects and overconfidence.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

London, UK
paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

Welcome to the first issue of the Journal for 2024, which begins with a research article by Klaus Reininger, Hannah Biel, Timo Hennig, Steffen Moritz, Peer Briken and Bernd Löwe entitled ‘Testing Heinz Kohut’s thoughts on narcissism and narcissistic rage: narcissistic injury paves the way for radicalization and subclinical paranoid states – an experimental study’. The authors identified a situation in Germany that would enable them to conduct research into the consequences of narcissistic injury. In 2019 German authorities began implementing a structural reform of psychotherapy training, with only a brief period of transition for those who were mid-training during the reforms. The authors conducted a randomised controlled experiment in which 100 psychology students were allocated to either a group that read information about how the short transition period would affect their own path to qualification as psychotherapists (experimental condition), or to a group that read about how the short transition period would affect education students hoping to qualify as child and adolescent psychotherapists (control condition). Both groups then also read the views of a professor who believed that effective, immediate action would be necessary to overturn the proposed short transition periods (a radicalization prompt). Following a 30 minute group discussion, participants in both conditions completed dependent measures of separatist identification, negative affects, overconfidence, radical intentions and subclinical paranoid states. Compared to the control condition, psychologists in the experimental condition experienced greater narcissistic injury. They experienced a stronger alloyance with an archaic omnipotent object, exhibited more negative affects, claimed overconfidence, more radical intentions and subclinical paranoid states. The authors suggest that these results support Kohut’s idea that narcissistic injuries can lead to antisocial and/or paranoid states mediated by the alloyance with an archaic-omnipotent object, negative affects and overconfidence.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinician Experiences of Remotely Delivering Psychodynamic and Intensive Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy within an NHS Institution During the COVID-19 Pandemic, and Possible Implications for Future Clinical Practice

Anna Croxford , Alan Baban , Sasha Kreel and Julian Stern

Julian Stern

London, UK
anna.croxford1@nhs.net

London, UK

London, UK

Julian Stern  

COVID-19, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy, remote

When it became clear that the COVID-19 pandemic would require significant shifts in working practice, staff in the Adult Complex Needs department at the Tavistock clinic designed a study to explore the emotional impact of remote working on clinicians and patients, and to consider related clinical implications both individually and collectively. A set of semi-structured questions were designed to tap into experiences of working remotely, and clinicians were interviewed. The material arising from these interviews has been summarised into themes. The predominant theme was clinicians’ awareness of the importance of their own emotional milieu in delivering psychoanalytic psychotherapy, especially at a time of crisis, in their initial emotional responses and as they gathered further remote experience. They raised challenges around maintaining an analytic frame amid therapeutic uncertainties, and in the context of the many physical losses of a remote frame: blurred clinical boundaries, altered emotional experiences and resonance, and important impacts on holding, patient attendance, and worsening distress and risks for patients who struggled remotely. Strategies to adapt were key, particularly remaining emotionally consistent and observant amidst uncertainty, and support was paramount to do so. These experiences assist in informing clinicians about the limitations and uses of remote clinical work.

responseSet.title: A Virtual Frame – How Does Forensic Psychotherapy Work Remotely?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Virtual Frame – How Does Forensic Psychotherapy Work Remotely?

Gemma Routledge

Gemma Routledge

London, UK
gjroutledge@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0009-0008-8776-8595

UK

Gemma Routledge  

forensic psychotherapy, remote video psychotherapy, telemental health

Psychotherapy has been practised via remote technology since the mid-1990s and has grown in mainstream popularity; yet, it remains controversial, particularly for psychoanalytically informed treatment, and limited research in this area has been published. However, COVID-19 provoked a necessary transition to online working across the psychotherapy profession, including clinicians providing psychoanalytic treatment to a unique forensic outpatient group remotely for the first time. This qualitative research study sought insights from this sample via grounded theory interviews and analysis to inform hypothetical future practice of remote psychotherapy via videoconferencing to forensic populations with limited access to treatment. This generated a substantive theory about how forensic psychotherapy works remotely: In the context of a forced transition to remote working, forensic psychotherapists make attempts to substitute for real by establishing an alternative frame; noticing how differently contact is mediated; adapting technique; and evaluating suitability for remote treatment. An implication of these findings may be that developing a new Virtual Frame for forensic psychotherapy, supported by specialist training and supervision, could increase its reach within settings that provide sufficient containment for the work. Further research is encouraged to support this undertaking and consider which patients, and/or clinicians, might work effectively this way.

[[RunningHead]]

Experience of Psychic Restructuring among Refugees in the Context of Life in Refuge

Riswana B and Baiju Gopal

Baiju Gopal

Bengaluru, India
riswananasweef@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1678-3658

Bengaluru, India
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8191-7045

Baiju Gopal  

Art-based therapy, exile, psychic restructuring, life in the refuge, first-generation

Changes in the inner voice, thoughts, and feelings due to the recurrent experiences in social and cultural reality are defined as psychic restructuring (PR). Any collective trauma can drastically change a person’s psyche in multiple ways. The first argument of the study is that PR happens in individuals as a result of exile, which has manifold reasons. The second argument is that first-generation older adults crave for their homeland more intensely because of their subtle memories of the left behind. The third argument is that there are metaphorical representations of exilic experiences in the art forms that would depict their inexplicable quest for PR. Finally, it suggests the possibility of constructive PR in refugees with the aid of clinically driven and standardized art-based therapies (ABT). These arguments have multicollinearity in the context of PR, which still needs to be studied. The study tries to bridge the gap or lacuna in this research arena.

[[RunningHead]]

Learning from the Patient in Retrospect: Patrick Casement’s Clinical Approach in an Extraordinary Paragraph

Marc Kingsley and Patrick Casement

Patrick Casement

Harley Therapy 10 Harley Street, London W1G 9PF, UK
marc.kingsley@nelft.nhs.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8363-927X

London, UK

Patrick Casement  

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy, learning from patients, unconscious communication, unconscious hope, trial identification

(Received 3 October 2023; accepted 17 February 2024)

Patrick Casement, renowned psychoanalyst, was recently encouraged by Dr Kelly Jayne, to write a paragraph describing his approach to clinical work. ‘Learning from the patient in retrospect’ was published in the Bulletin of the British Psychoanalytical Society June 2023 (vol 59 no 5) page 31. As the Bulletin is confidential, and only for members of the British Psychoanalytical Society, we are pleased to share it to a wider audience here, with kind permission of the author and publisher.

‘Now approaching 88, I had thought I would be immune to further temptations to write. But I was mistaken. When asked to describe my approach to clinical work “in just one paragraph”, how could I resist such a challenge? So, here goes.

Learning from the Patient in Retrospect

While listening to a patient, there are some questions which I am putting to myself. What might the patient most need me to hear in what they are communicating to me at this moment, whether in word or silence, or in the quality of their presence? What can I say that could open up some exploring of this? What might I then be contributing to the analytic space and process? Could this then lead to creative playfulness or might it just lead to some confrontation or compliance? Am I merely applying insight from preconception or am I opening up a joint exploring towards some fresh understanding, which could be arrived at along with the patient? Am I trying to analyse from some position of assumed understanding, or am I truly open to arriving at some insight along with the patient? Am I actually open to learning with my patient at this moment or am I slipping back into trying to interpret from theory and from other experience?’

The paragraph was warmly received, with many colleagues sharing generous feedback some of which we include below.
